plainMeaning: Saying Lord Pārśvanātha's name aloud calms every great danger — disease, drowning, fire, snakebite, robbers, foes, lions, elephants and war.
recitation: roga jala jalaṇa visahara, corāri maïnda gaya raṇa bhayāiṁ; pāsajiṇa-nāma-saṅkittaṇeṇa pasamanti savvāiṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. roga (√ruj, 'to break/afflict'); Prakrit roga unchanged.
wordByWord.notes: First item of the recapitulated danger-list; the leprosy/disease peril of vv.2–3.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jala, 'water'; here by metonymy the shipwreck/sea danger of vv.4–5.
Prakrit jalaṇa = Skt. jvalana (√jval, 'to blaze'); the forest-fire of vv.6–7.
wordByWord.notes: Note the near-rhyme jala / jalaṇa — water set beside fire.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit visahara = Skt. viṣa-dhara, 'venom-bearer'; the snakebite peril of vv.8–9.
wordByWord.notes: Preface-glossed term; used here without re-definition.

wordByWord.etymology: Dvandva: cora ('thief', Skt. cora) + ari ('enemy', Skt. ari); the bandit peril of vv.10–11 plus the hostile foe.
wordByWord.notes: Two perils folded into one word for metrical economy.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit maïnda = Skt. mṛgendra, 'lord of beasts' (mṛga + indra); the lion extension of vv.12–15.
Prakrit gaya = Skt. gaja, 'elephant'; the rutting-elephant peril of vv.12–15.
Skt. raṇa, 'combat'; the battle peril of vv.16–17.
Skt. bhayāni, nom./acc. neut. pl. of bhaya, 'fear'; Prakrit -āiṁ for -āni.
wordByWord.notes: Sums the eight/nine preceding nouns as 'these fears'.

wordByWord.etymology: pāsa (Skt. pārśva) + jiṇa (Skt. jina, 'victor'); a tatpuruṣa naming the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara.
wordByWord.notes: Genitive in sense ('of Pārśva-jina'), governing nāma.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. nāman, 'name'; here the holy name treated as efficacious sound.
Prakrit saṅkittaṇa = Skt. saṅkīrtana (sam + √kīrt, 'to proclaim, praise'), instr. sg. -eṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Audible utterance — distinct from silent smaraṇa; the verse's operative means.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pasamanti = Skt. praśāmyanti (pra + √śam, 'to be quelled'), 3rd pl. pres.
wordByWord.notes: Intransitive: the fears settle rather than being slain.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savvāiṁ = Skt. sarvāṇi, nom. neut. pl. of sarva, 'all'.
wordByWord.notes: Totalizing word that seals the claim — no listed peril exempt.

literalTranslation: Disease, water, fire, serpent, thieves-and-foes, lion, elephant, battle — [these] fears: by the proclaiming of the name of Pārśva the Jina, all are stilled.
idiomaticTranslation: Disease, drowning, fire and venomous serpent; robbers and foes, the lion, the rutting elephant, and the clash of battle — every one of these terrors is wholly quelled by simply proclaiming aloud the name of Pārśva-jina.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After seven danger-pairs each unfolded at leisure, verse 18 does the opposite: it compresses the whole catalogue into one breathless run — roga, jala, jalaṇa, visahara, corāri, maïnda, gaya, raṇa — eight perils (nine, if corāri is split) named without a single connective, piled so the line itself races like the dangers it invokes. The pivot is the instrumental nāma-saṅkittaṇeṇa: not the 'feet' (calaṇa) of the invocation-verses, nor silent recollection, but saṅkīrtana — the Name spoken, sounded, proclaimed aloud. The verb pasamanti ('they subside, are stilled') is intransitive: the fears are not slain or put to flight so much as simply settled, the way agitation settles into praśama (calm). And savvāiṁ — 'all of them' — seals the claim: nothing in the preceding litany is exempt. Where each earlier pair argued one rescue at a time, this verse asserts the whole by recapitulation, the gathering gāthā that lets the litany close before the hymn turns (v.19) to praise itself. Note the poet's economy: corāri folds thief (cora) and foe (ari) into one word, and maïnda (mṛgendra, 'lord of beasts') crowns the lion — the line deliberately crowded so its sound enacts dangers thronging, then all at once quelled by one uttered Name.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on nāma-smaraṇa raised to nāma-saṅkīrtana: the Jina's name is treated not as a mere label but as efficacious sound. Crucially, Jain devotion locates the power not in Pārśva acting — a liberated siddha does not intervene in the world — but in the purifying effect on the one who recites. Fear (bhaya) is a mode of mohanīya-driven agitation; praśama, its stilling, is itself a rung of the path. So 'all subside' is psychological and spiritual before it is circumstantial: the Name works by quieting the mind that magnifies every peril, which is precisely why the intransitive pasamanti, not a transitive 'destroys', is the right verb.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: At any moment of acute fear — sudden illness, an accident, a threat on the road — the reciter is taught to do exactly what the verse names: say Pārśva's name aloud, audibly, rather than merely think it. The deliberate shift from silent worry to vocal saṅkīrtana is itself the technique; the steadied breath and settled mind are the 'subsiding' the verse promises.

crossReferences.text: Namiūṇa Bhayahara Stotra vv.2–17 — the seven danger-pairs this verse gathers and recapitulates
Upasargahara Stotra (4th Smaraṇa) — parallel Pārśva fear-removal ('visahara-phuliṅga-manta')

sources.title: Parshvanatha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Pārśva as the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara identified by the serpent-hood, and Dharaṇendra association, confirming the addressee of pāsajiṇa-nāma

sources.title: Mrigendra — definition (Wisdomlib Sanskrit/Marathi lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: confirming maïnda = Skt. mṛgendra ('lord of beasts', lion)

sources.title: Samkirtana / Saṅkīrtana — definition (Wisdomlib)
sources.consultedFor: confirming saṅkittaṇa = Skt. saṅkīrtana ('proclamation, praise aloud') vs. silent smaraṇa

notes: The verse is the summary gāthā (v.18) that recapitulates the hymn's catalogue of great fears. Word-readings follow the supplied source verbatim (e.g. જલણ jalaṇa, મઇંદ maïnda). 'corāri' is parsed as a dvandva cora+ari (thief + foe); some recensions and reciters treat it as a single 'thieves-and-enemies' item, which shifts the peril count between eight and nine — the totalizing savvāiṁ ('all') is unaffected either way. AI-generated web summaries of this stotra were found unreliable (one conflated it with Naminātha and fabricated Devanāgarī lines); accordingly only per-word lexical meanings were taken from dictionaries and the doctrinal framing from the supplied knowledge base, not from those summaries. The pāsajiṇa-nāma compound carries an implicit genitive ('of Pārśva-jina'). pasamanti is intransitive ('subside'), deliberately not transitive 'destroys'.
